user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Malayalam (code=ml, script=Malayalam). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 26
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Kerala
- district: Thrissur
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: A 26-year-old homemaker from Thrissur, Kerala, currently married, with a higher secondary education and Malayalam as her first language. She resides in a rural area and manages household finances with structured budgeting. Her lifestyle includes regular yoga and community involvement, reflecting a disciplined and community-oriented routine.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|മാതാ ആശുപത്രി]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-KL-01]]
രോഗി: [[PATIENT_NAME|അനിത ശർമ്മ]] | DOB [[DOB|1998-05-22]] | പ്രായം: [[AGE|26]] | ലിംഗം: [[GENDER|Female]]
തൊഴിൽ: [[OCCUPATION|അധ്യാപിക]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | ഡോക്ടർ: [[DOCTOR_NAME|ഡോ. രാജേഷ് കുമാർ]]
ബന്ധു: [[RELATIVE_NAME|പ്രവീൺ ശർമ്മ]] | ഫോൺ: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
രജിസ്ട്രാർ EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-8821]] | ജില്ല: [[DISTRICT|കൊല്ലം]]
പ്രധാന പരാതി: 3 ദിവസമായി വലതുവശത്തെ അടിവയറ്റിൽ വേദനയോടു കൂടിയ വീക്കവും പനിയും.

പരിശോധന:
വയറ്: വലതുവശത്തെ താഴെ ഭാഗത്ത് വേദന, പേശികൾ മുറുകുന്ന അവസ്ഥ, 5x4 സെ.മീ വലിപ്പമുള്ള മാറ്റമില്ലാത്ത മുഴ സ്പർശിച്ചറിയാൻ സാധിക്കുന്നു. മറ്റ് ഭാഗങ്ങളിൽ പേശികൾ മുറുകുന്നില്ല. കുടലിന്റെ ശബ്ദങ്ങൾ സാധാരണ നിലയിലാണ്.

പരിശോധനകൾ:
- വയറിന്റെ അൾട്രാസൗണ്ട്: അപ്പെൻഡിക്സ് ഭാഗത്ത് വീക്കവും ചുറ്റുമുള്ള ഭാഗത്ത് ദ്രാവക ശേഖരണവും.
- CBC: WBC കൗണ്ട് 14,000/cu mm, ന്യൂട്രോഫീലിയ.

പദ്ധതി:
1. IV ആന്റിബയോട്ടിക്കുകൾ നൽകി പ്രവേശനം ഉറപ്പാക്കുക, കൂടുതൽ പരിശോധനകൾ നടത്തുക.
2. അപ്പെൻഡിക്സ് നീക്കം ചെയ്യുന്നതിനായി ശസ്ത്രക്രിയാ വിഭാഗത്തിന്റെ അഭിപ്രായം തേടുക.
3. രക്തത്തിലെ പഞ്ചസാര പരിശോധിച്ചു, അത് സാധാരണ നിലയിലാണ്.
4. NPO പാലിക്കാൻ നിർദ്ദേശിച്ചു.
5. വേദന കുറയ്ക്കുന്നതിനുള്ള മരുന്നുകൾ നൽകി.
6. 2 ദിവസത്തിന് ശേഷം വീണ്ടും വരിക, ലക്ഷണങ്ങൾ വർദ്ധിക്കുകയാണെങ്കിൽ അതിനുമുമ്പ് വരിക.

Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Nair]] | DOB [[DOB|1998-05-22]] | Age: [[AGE|26]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sreekumar Menon]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Vinod Nair]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-8821]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Thrissur]]
Chief complaint: Painful swelling in right lower abdomen for 3 days with fever.

Examination:
Abdomen: Right lower quadrant tender, guarding present, 5x4 cm non-reducible mass palpable. No guarding in other quadrants. Bowel sounds present.

Investigations:
- Ultrasound Abdomen: Appendix mass with periappendiceal collection.
- CBC: WBC count 14,000/cu mm with neutrophilia.

Plan:
1. Admit for IV antibiotics and further evaluation.
2. Surgical consult for possible appendectomy.
3. Blood sugar checked, found to be normal.
4. Advised to keep NPO.
5. Prescribed analgesics for pain management.
6. Follow-up in 2 days or earlier if symptoms worsen.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 26
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Kerala
- district: Thrissur
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: A 26-year-old female homemaker from Thrissur, Kerala, married, with higher secondary education, fluent in Malayalam and basic English. She manages household finances meticulously and follows a structured daily routine. Her lifestyle includes regular yoga and community involvement, with a cultural background rooted in traditional Kerala customs and Christian practices.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]

Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]

- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]

[[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|അന്ന മേരി, നാലുകവല, കൊച്ചി]]
[[DISTRICT|എറണാകുളം]]

പ്രധാന പരാതി:
രോഗിക്ക് 5 ദിവസമായി പനി, ശരീരവേദന, തലവേദന എന്നിവയുണ്ട്. അവർക്ക് 100.4°F കുറഞ്ഞ പനിയും, ശരീരത്തിലാകെ പേശിവേദനയും, ഉണങ്ങിയ ചുമയുമുണ്ട്. അവർക്ക് തൊണ്ടവേദനയും ക്ഷീണവും അനുഭവപ്പെടുന്നു. ശ്വാസംമുട്ടലോ, നെഞ്ചുവേദനയോ, ഛർദ്ദിയോ ഇല്ലെന്ന് അവർ പറയുന്നു. ഒരു കുടുംബ സംഗമത്തിൽ പങ്കെടുത്തതിന് ശേഷമാണ് ലക്ഷണങ്ങൾ തുടങ്ങിയതെന്ന് അവർ സൂചിപ്പിച്ചു.

കണ്ടെത്തലുകൾ:
പരിശോധനയിൽ രോഗി ബോധവതിയും സാധാരണ നിലയിലുള്ളവളുമാണ്. വൈറ്റൽസ്: താപനില 100.6°F, പൾസ് 98/മിനിറ്റ്, രക്തസമ്മർദ്ദം 110/70 mmHg, ശ്വസന നിരക്ക് 18/മിനിറ്റ്, മുറിയിലെ വായുവിൽ SpO2 98%. പൊതുവായ പരിശോധനയിൽ പ്രത്യേക മാറ്റങ്ങളൊന്നുമില്ല. ശ്വാസകോശ പരിശോധനയിൽ ഇരുവശത്തും വ്യക്തമായ ശ്വസനശബ്ദങ്ങൾ കേൾക്കാം. വയറ് മൃദുവായതും വേദനയില്ലാത്തതുമാണ്. അവയവങ്ങൾ വീങ്ങിയിട്ടില്ല.

പദ്ധതി:
1. പനിക്കും ശരീരവേദനയ്ക്കും ആവശ്യാനുസരണം ഓരോ 6 മണിക്കൂറിലും 500mg പാരസെറ്റമോൾ ഗുളിക.
2. 3 ദിവസത്തേക്ക് ദിവസവും ഒരു തവണ 250mg അസിത്രോമൈസിൻ ഗുളിക.
3. പനിക്കു വേണ്ടി ഓരോ 8 മണിക്കൂറിലും 5ml ക്രോസിൻ അഡ്വാൻസ് സിറപ്പ് (പാരസെറ്റമോൾ 250mg/5ml).
4. ധാരാളം വിശ്രമവും വെള്ളവും കുടിക്കുക.
5. 3 ദിവസത്തിന് ശേഷം വീണ്ടും പരിശോധിക്കുക, ലക്ഷണങ്ങൾ വഷളായാൽ അതിനുമുമ്പും വരാവുന്നതാണ്.

ഡോ. തോമസ് ഫിലിപ്പ്
ഒപ്പ്: T.P.

Chief Complaint:
Patient presents with a 5-day history of fever, body ache, and headache. She reports a low-grade fever of 100.4°F, generalized myalgia, and a non-productive cough. She also complains of mild sore throat and fatigue. She denies any shortness of breath, chest pain, or vomiting. She mentions that her symptoms started after attending a family gathering.

Findings:
On examination, the patient is conscious and oriented. Vitals: Temperature 100.6°F, Pulse 98/min, Blood Pressure 110/70 mmHg, Respiratory Rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air. General examination is unremarkable. Chest examination reveals clear breath sounds bilaterally. Abdomen is soft and non-tender. No organomegaly is palpable.

Plan:
1.  Paracetamol 500mg tablet, one tablet every 6 hours as needed for fever and body ache.
2.  Tablet Azithromycin 250mg, one tablet once daily for 3 days.
3.  Syrup Crocin Advance (Paracetamol 250mg/5ml), 5ml every 8 hours for fever.
4.  Adequate rest and hydration with plenty of fluids.
5.  Follow up in 3 days or sooner if symptoms worsen.

Dr. Thomas Philip

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.

Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female

Her first language is Manipuri, and she has functional literacy in English. Clinically, she may have dietary habits centered on traditional Manipuri foods like Eromba and Singju.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]

Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:

- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.

generated_text:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Bishnupur]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-MN-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Thokchom Sanjita Devi]] | DOB [[DOB|1991-08-22]] | Age: [[AGE|33]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0822-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. L. Renu Sharma]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Thokchom Sanjoy Singh]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-BHR-045]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Bishnupur]]
Chief complaint: Recurrent episodes of burning sensation during micturition for the past two weeks.

History of present illness: Patient reports a history of dysuria and frequency of urination, which she describes as a burning sensation. Symptoms are more pronounced in the evenings and are not associated with fever or chills. She has tried some over-the-counter analgesics with minimal relief. She denies any history of similar episodes in the past.

Examination findings:
Vitals: BP 120/80 mmHg, Pulse 88/min, Temperature 98.4°F, SpO2 99% on room air.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no guarding or rebound.
Renal: No costovertebral angle tenderness.
Urine: Sample sent for routine microscopy and culture.

Impression:
1. Uncomplicated urinary tract infection (UTI).

Plan:
1. Start empirical antibiotic therapy with Nitrofurantoin 100mg twice daily for 5 days.
2. Advise adequate fluid intake, preferably water.
3. Counsel on hygiene practices.
4. Follow-up in 3 days or sooner if symptoms worsen.
5. Await urine culture and sensitivity report.

professional_persona: Chao, a 33‑year‑old home‑based culinary and craft entrepreneur, blends their expertise in Manipuri cooking, bamboo‑weaving, and household management with a gregarious, improvisational style, though they often find it challenging to stick to strict schedules.
linguistic_persona: Chao, fluent in Manipuri (Meitei) and comfortable reading both Meitei Mayek and Bengali scripts, can navigate everyday English signage and write short notes, while they understand basic Hindi from television.
sports_persona: Chao follows NEROCA FC in the I‑League, cheers for the Indian women’s hockey squad, and stays fit with early morning walks and occasional community yoga sessions, though they rarely play organized sports themselves.
arts_persona: Chao immerses themselves in Manipuri folk dance, enjoys listening to A.R. Rahman’s film scores, sings along to Lata Mangeshkar’s timeless bhajans, and follows regional actress Bala Hijam’s movies.
travel_persona: Chao enjoys weekend trips to nearby Imphal markets and occasional trips to Shillong’s waterfalls, while they dream of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a beach holiday in Goa.
culinary_persona: Chao loves cooking Eromba with fermented fish, Singju with mustard leaves and bamboo shoots, and sweet Chakhao kheer, while they also experiment with paneer tikka and use fresh coriander, ginger and green chilies in every dish.
persona: Chao, a vivacious 33‑year‑old village entrepreneur, blends curiosity‑driven culinary experiments with community‑centered craft sales, while they juggle worries and a penchant for spontaneous social gatherings.
cultural_background: In Bishnupur, Manipur, Chao is part of the Meitei community, which blends Hindu Vaishnavite traditions with rich indigenous customs. Rural life revolves around agriculture, family farms, and communal festivals such as Yaoshang (the Manipuri Holi) and Cheiraoba (New Year). The village’s close‑knit social fabric encourages collective activities, from folk dance rehearsals to temple celebrations, fostering a strong sense of shared identity and mutual support.
linguistic_background: Chao's mother‑tongue is Manipuri (Meitei), which she speaks fluently and can read and write using the Meitei Mayek script as well as the Bengali script taught in school. She completed middle school, giving her functional literacy in English – she can read simple signs, recipes and letters, and write everyday notes. Hindi is not a language she uses regularly, though she can understand some spoken Hindi from television and can recognise basic Devanagari letters.
skills_and_expertise: Chao excels at managing a busy household, preparing traditional Manipuri dishes, and caring for children and elders. Her strong interpersonal skills, honed by her talkative and sociable nature, make her an effective mediator in family matters and community gatherings. She is adept at sewing, weaving bamboo crafts, and coordinating local festivals, often improvising solutions under time pressure. Additionally, her curiosity drives her to experiment with new recipes and simple DIY projects, while her practical mindset ensures they are functional and affordable.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Manipuri culinary arts", "Childcare", "Elderly care", "Sewing and textile crafts", "Bamboo craft making", "Community festival coordination", "Conflict mediation", "Creative problem solving", "DIY project experimentation"]
hobbies_and_interests: Chao loves spending her free time socialising with neighbours over tea, dancing to traditional Manipuri folk songs, and experimenting with new recipes in her kitchen. She enjoys tending a small vegetable garden, weaving bamboo mats, and listening to devotional music on the radio. On weekends she often visits the local market to browse handicrafts, watches Bollywood and regional movies, and reads stories from the Mahabharata and local legends.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Socializing over tea with neighbours", "Traditional Manipuri folk dancing", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Vegetable gardening", "Bamboo weaving", "Listening to devotional music", "Visiting local markets", "Watching Bollywood and regional movies", "Reading mythological stories (Mahabharata, local legends)"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Chao aims to turn her expertise in Manipuri cooking and handicrafts into a small home‑based enterprise that supplies ready‑made meals and handcrafted items to nearby towns. She wishes to pursue a short certification in basic accounting and digital marketing to manage the venture effectively. Over the next few years, she hopes to mentor other rural women, organize skill‑sharing workshops, and eventually facilitate a community cooperative that empowers households financially. Simultaneously, she wants to improve her English proficiency to navigate government schemes and support her children's higher education.
persona_clinical_summary: A 33-year-old female currently married individual from rural Manipur, she has a middle education level and speaks Manipuri as her first language. She is a homemaker with no formal occupation, and her lifestyle includes community-centered activities and a preference for traditional culinary practices. Her cultural background is rooted in the Meitei community, blending indigenous customs with local traditions.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

professional_persona: Sarsati Patra is a budding folk‑art entrepreneur who weaves handloom textiles and paints traditional Pattachitra, using their curiosity and community‑driven spirit to launch a modest home‑based studio that supports their family.
linguistic_persona: Sarsati Patra is fluent in Odia, manages basic Hindi gleaned from television and radio, and can spot common English words on their phone, though they struggle with extended conversation.
sports_persona: Sarsati Patra enjoys evening cricket matches with neighbourhood friends, follows the Kolkata Knight Riders in the IPL, occasionally joins a women's kabaddi club at the village school, and prefers group sports that blend fitness with social camaraderie.
arts_persona: Sarsati Patra finds inspiration in the rhythmic chants of Odissi maestro Kelucharan Mohapatra, studies the vibrant Pattachitra canvases of Raghunath Behera, and unwinds to Bollywood hits featuring Madhuri Dixit, weaving these influences into their own sketches and woven designs.
travel_persona: Sarsati Patra loves spontaneous weekend getaways to the historic Konark Sun Temple and the sandy Puri shoreline, dreams of taking a pilgrimage to Varanasi’s ghats, and hopes to someday explore the vibrant streets of Bangkok and the serene beaches of Bali.
culinary_persona: Sarsati Patra excels at preparing classic Odia dishes such as dalma, pakhala bhata, chhena poda, and mustard‑spiced roti, relishes the tangy bite of sambhar with kokum, and often enjoys al‑fresco meals at the village beach stalls whenever possible.
persona: Sarsati Patra is a lively 20‑year‑old artisan who juggles temple sketches, kitchen experiments, and spontaneous beach walks, often losing track of time but always greeting friends with a bright smile.
cultural_background: Sarsati hails from the coastal rural belt of Puri in Odisha, a region steeped in Jagannath temple traditions, vibrant folk arts such as Pattachitra and Odissi dance, and a close‑knit village life where community gatherings revolve around festivals and agricultural cycles.
linguistic_background: Her mother tongue is Odia; she reads and writes the Odia script fluently thanks to her primary schooling and daily use at home. While she has had no formal schooling in Hindi or English, she picks up basic Hindi from television and radio, allowing her to understand simple conversations. Her exposure to English is limited to occasional exposure via mobile phones and cartoons, so she can recognize common words but cannot hold a fluent dialogue.
skills_and_expertise: She is skilled at preparing traditional Odia meals, handloom weaving, singing devotional folk songs, recounting local legends, performing basic arithmetic for household budgeting, coordinating village festivals, and tending a kitchen garden.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Cooking traditional Odia dishes', 'Handloom weaving', 'Folk singing (bhajans)', 'Storytelling', 'Basic arithmetic', 'Community event coordination', 'Kitchen gardening']
hobbies_and_interests: She enjoys listening to Odissi music and devotional bhajans, dancing during festivals, sketching temple‑inspired artworks, experimenting with new recipes in the kitchen, collecting seashells on the beach, playing group games with friends, and participating in storytelling circles where she shares folk tales.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Odissi music', 'Dancing during festivals', 'Sketching temple-inspired art', 'Experimenting with new recipes', 'Collecting seashells at the beach', 'Playing group games with friends', 'Participating in storytelling circles']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Sarsati dreams of mastering Odia folk art techniques like Pattachitra and improving her handloom weaving, aiming to open a modest home‑based studio that sells handcrafted paintings and woven textiles to support her family and preserve her cultural heritage. She also wishes to attend government‑run skill‑development workshops to boost her literacy and eventually become a mentor for younger girls in her village.
persona_clinical_summary: A 20-year-old female from Puri, Odisha, who is never married and retired, with primary education and a rural background. Her first language is Odia, and she has limited exposure to Hindi or English. She leads a lifestyle centered around traditional arts and community activities.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

user_prompt:
Write one complete Insurance Claim / TPA Form for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 20
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Puri
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: A 20-year-old female from Puri, Odisha, who is never married and retired, with primary education and a rural background. Her first language is Odia, and she has limited exposure to Hindi or English. She leads a lifestyle centered around traditional arts and community activities.

Document format guide for Insurance Claim / TPA Form (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

judge_reasoning: All narrative prose in English; expected Odia script for clinical content per instructions.

professional_persona: Swarna Kanr, a retired village elder and cultural custodian, channels her decades of expertise in traditional household management, Phulkari embroidery, and folk storytelling into mentoring grandchildren, balancing meticulous organization with a vivid imagination and a gentle yet assertive demeanor, and they bring the same disciplined creativity to every community activity they join.
linguistic_persona: Swarna Kanr, a lifelong oral storyteller, speaks fluent Punjabi, understands spoken Hindi in daily conversations, yet cannot read Gurmukhi, Hindi or English scripts, relying on memory and auditory learning for all communication, and they cherish the power of spoken word above written text.
sports_persona: Swarna Kanr enjoys watching Kabaddi matches on TV, follows the Indian Premier League and roots for the Delhi Capitals, takes a leisurely morning walk around the village field, and occasionally joins community Giddha sessions as a lively form of exercise.
arts_persona: Swarna Kanr passionately weaves intricate Phulkari embroidery on cloth, leads spirited Giddha dances during village celebrations, sings devotional bhajans each sunrise, and cherishes listening to legendary folk singers like Surinder Kaur and Jagjit Singh.
travel_persona: Swarna Kanr prefers short pilgrimages to nearby holy sites like Amritsar’s Golden Temple and seasonal trips to Shimla for fresh air, plans trips well in advance with family, travels by train or shared auto, and avoids long‑haul flights due to health concerns.
culinary_persona: Swarna Kanr expertly prepares makki di roti, sarson da saag, and buttery lassi, enjoys the aromatic blend of mustard greens and jaggery, prefers home‑grown vegetables from her kitchen garden, and while she no longer cooks daily, she delights in sharing her recipes during family gatherings.
persona: Swarna Kanr, a vivacious 73‑year‑old keeper of oral tales and Phulkari art, balances frugal living with generous festival splurges, manages health aches with herbal remedies, and radiates curiosity through endless storytelling.
cultural_background: Swarna hails from a small village near Rupnagar in the rural heartland of Punjab. She belongs to a Punjabi Hindu family that has lived in the region for generations, steeped in Punjabi folk traditions, agricultural life, and community festivals such as Baisakhi, Lohri and Teeyan. Her household follows joint‑family customs, with strong emphasis on hospitality, respect for elders and the preservation of oral history.
linguistic_background: Swarna's mother tongue is Punjabi; she speaks Punjabi fluently and understands its nuances, idioms and proverbs. Because she never attended school, she cannot read or write Gurmukhi, Hindi or English. She can follow simple spoken Hindi in daily conversations and understands basic instructions from television or neighbours, but she does not read Hindi script. Her linguistic skills are rooted in oral tradition, storytelling and memorised devotional chants.
skills_and_expertise: Swarna excels in managing a Punjabi household, preparing traditional dishes such as makki di roti, sarson da saag, and lassi. She is proficient in Phulkari embroidery, creating intricate needlework for family shawls. She leads Giddha dance performances during festivals, sings devotional bhajans, and narrates folk stories (kisse) to younger relatives. She knows home remedies using local herbs, cultivates a kitchen garden with seasonal vegetables, and mediates family disputes with calm authority.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Punjabi cooking", "Phulkari embroidery", "Giddha dance", "Bhajan singing", "Oral storytelling (kisse)", "Herbal home remedies", "Kitchen gardening", "Family budgeting", "Community dispute mediation"]
hobbies_and_interests: Swarna enjoys listening to Punjabi folk music on the radio, singing bhajans during early mornings, and dancing Giddha at community gatherings. She spends afternoons tending to her kitchen garden, weaving phulkari on small cloth pieces, and recounting folk tales to grandchildren. She also likes watching devotional TV shows and occasionally knitting simple shawls for charity.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Punjabi folk music", "Singing bhajans", "Giddha dancing", "Kitchen gardening", "Phulkari stitching", "Storytelling to grandchildren", "Watching devotional TV shows", "Knitting simple shawls"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired, Swarna aims to preserve her family’s cultural heritage by teaching her grandchildren Punjabi cooking, Phulkari stitching and folk songs. She wishes to document several family kisse on audio recordings for future generations. She also hopes to stay socially active by volunteering at the village temple’s senior volunteers’ group and learning basic smartphone use to keep in touch with distant relatives.
persona_clinical_summary: A 73-year-old female from rural Punjab, currently married, with no formal occupation and illiterate education. She speaks Punjabi fluently and follows simple spoken Hindi. Swarna manages health with herbal remedies and has a history of frugal living with occasional festival splurges.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ਫੋਨ [[PHONE_NUMBER|+91-9876543210]] | [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-RGN-001]] | [[ABHA_ID|ABHA-1234-5678-9012]]
ਖੋਜਾਂ: ਖੋਪੜੀ ਦਾ ਸਧਾਰਨ ਰੇਡੀਓਗ੍ਰਾਫ ਕੋਈ ਗੰਭੀਰ ਇੰਟਰਾਕਰੇਨੀਅਲ ਹੈਮਰੇਜ, ਮਾਸ ਇਫੈਕਟ, ਜਾਂ ਖੋਪੜੀ ਦੇ ਫ੍ਰੈਕਚਰ ਨੂੰ ਨਹੀਂ ਦਰਸਾਉਂਦਾ। ਪੈਰਾਨਾਸਲ ਸਾਈਨਸ ਸਾਫ਼ ਹਨ। ਹੱਡੀਆਂ ਦੀਆਂ ਸੰਰਚਨਾਵਾਂ ਸਹੀ ਲੱਗਦੀਆਂ ਹਨ। ਹੱਡੀਆਂ ਦੀ ਕਿਸੇ ਵੀ ਗੰਭੀਰ ਅਸਧਾਰਨਤਾ ਦਾ ਕੋਈ ਸਬੂਤ ਨਹੀਂ ਹੈ।

ਯੋਜਨਾ: ਕਲੀਨਿਕਲ ਤੌਰ 'ਤੇ ਸਹਿ-ਸੰਬੰਧਿਤ ਕਰੋ। ਕੋਈ ਗੰਭੀਰ ਰੇਡੀਓਗ੍ਰਾਫਿਕ ਅਸਧਾਰਨਤਾ ਨਹੀਂ ਪਛਾਣੀ ਗਈ। ਇਲਾਜ ਕਰਨ ਵਾਲੇ ਮਨੋਵਿਗਿਆਨੀ ਨਾਲ ਕਲੀਨਿਕਲ ਪ੍ਰਬੰਧਨ ਲਈ ਫਾਲੋ-ਅਪ ਦੀ ਸਿਫਾਰਸ਼ ਕੀਤੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ।
judge_reasoning: Male-coded name (Jaswant Singh) + female gender tag; district Amritsar vs expected Rupnagar; minor domain fit issue for psych+radiology

professional_persona: Swarna Kanr, a retired village elder and cultural custodian, channels her decades of expertise in traditional household management, Phulkari embroidery, and folk storytelling into mentoring grandchildren, balancing meticulous organization with a vivid imagination and a gentle yet assertive demeanor, and they bring the same disciplined creativity to every community activity they join.
linguistic_persona: Swarna Kanr, a lifelong oral storyteller, speaks fluent Punjabi, understands spoken Hindi in daily conversations, yet cannot read Gurmukhi, Hindi or English scripts, relying on memory and auditory learning for all communication, and they cherish the power of spoken word above written text.
sports_persona: Swarna Kanr enjoys watching Kabaddi matches on TV, follows the Indian Premier League and roots for the Delhi Capitals, takes a leisurely morning walk around the village field, and occasionally joins community Giddha sessions as a lively form of exercise.
arts_persona: Swarna Kanr passionately weaves intricate Phulkari embroidery on cloth, leads spirited Giddha dances during village celebrations, sings devotional bhajans each sunrise, and cherishes listening to legendary folk singers like Surinder Kaur and Jagjit Singh.
travel_persona: Swarna Kanr prefers short pilgrimages to nearby holy sites like Amritsar’s Golden Temple and seasonal trips to Shimla for fresh air, plans trips well in advance with family, travels by train or shared auto, and avoids long‑haul flights due to health concerns.
culinary_persona: Swarna Kanr expertly prepares makki di roti, sarson da saag, and buttery lassi, enjoys the aromatic blend of mustard greens and jaggery, prefers home‑grown vegetables from her kitchen garden, and while she no longer cooks daily, she delights in sharing her recipes during family gatherings.
persona: Swarna Kanr, a vivacious 73‑year‑old keeper of oral tales and Phulkari art, balances frugal living with generous festival splurges, manages health aches with herbal remedies, and radiates curiosity through endless storytelling.
cultural_background: Swarna hails from a small village near Rupnagar in the rural heartland of Punjab. She belongs to a Punjabi Hindu family that has lived in the region for generations, steeped in Punjabi folk traditions, agricultural life, and community festivals such as Baisakhi, Lohri and Teeyan. Her household follows joint‑family customs, with strong emphasis on hospitality, respect for elders and the preservation of oral history.
linguistic_background: Swarna's mother tongue is Punjabi; she speaks Punjabi fluently and understands its nuances, idioms and proverbs. Because she never attended school, she cannot read or write Gurmukhi, Hindi or English. She can follow simple spoken Hindi in daily conversations and understands basic instructions from television or neighbours, but she does not read Hindi script. Her linguistic skills are rooted in oral tradition, storytelling and memorised devotional chants.
skills_and_expertise: Swarna excels in managing a Punjabi household, preparing traditional dishes such as makki di roti, sarson da saag, and lassi. She is proficient in Phulkari embroidery, creating intricate needlework for family shawls. She leads Giddha dance performances during festivals, sings devotional bhajans, and narrates folk stories (kisse) to younger relatives. She knows home remedies using local herbs, cultivates a kitchen garden with seasonal vegetables, and mediates family disputes with calm authority.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Punjabi cooking", "Phulkari embroidery", "Giddha dance", "Bhajan singing", "Oral storytelling (kisse)", "Herbal home remedies", "Kitchen gardening", "Family budgeting", "Community dispute mediation"]
hobbies_and_interests: Swarna enjoys listening to Punjabi folk music on the radio, singing bhajans during early mornings, and dancing Giddha at community gatherings. She spends afternoons tending to her kitchen garden, weaving phulkari on small cloth pieces, and recounting folk tales to grandchildren. She also likes watching devotional TV shows and occasionally knitting simple shawls for charity.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Punjabi folk music", "Singing bhajans", "Giddha dancing", "Kitchen gardening", "Phulkari stitching", "Storytelling to grandchildren", "Watching devotional TV shows", "Knitting simple shawls"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired, Swarna aims to preserve her family’s cultural heritage by teaching her grandchildren Punjabi cooking, Phulkari stitching and folk songs. She wishes to document several family kisse on audio recordings for future generations. She also hopes to stay socially active by volunteering at the village temple’s senior volunteers’ group and learning basic smartphone use to keep in touch with distant relatives.
persona_clinical_summary: A 73-year-old female from rural Punjab, currently married, with no formal occupation and illiterate education. She speaks Punjabi as her first language and follows a traditional lifestyle with limited formal health management. Her daily routine includes herbal remedies for aches and community-based activities.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Punjabi (code=pa, script=Gurmukhi). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 73
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Punjab
- district: Rupnagar
- first_language: Punjabi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 73-year-old female from rural Punjab, currently married, with no formal occupation and illiterate education. She speaks Punjabi as her first language and follows a traditional lifestyle with limited formal health management. Her daily routine includes herbal remedies for aches and community-based activities.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

[[PATIENT_NAME|ਗੁਰਮੀਤ ਕੌਰ]] [[AGE|73]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|05]]

Vitals / acuity: ਮਰੀਜ਼ ਸੁਚੇਤ ਹੈ ਪਰ ਥਕਾਵਟ ਮਹਿਸੂਸ ਕਰ ਰਿਹਾ ਹੈ। BP 150/90 mmHg, HR 98 bpm, RR 20/min, SpO2 96% on room air, Temp 101.2°F. Pain score 6/10 in lower abdomen. ਮਰੀਜ਼ ਨੇ ਚੱਕਰ ਆਉਣ ਅਤੇ ਕਮਜ਼ੋਰੀ ਦੀ ਰਿਪੋਰਟ ਕੀਤੀ ਹੈ।

Nursing Notes:
ਮਰੀਜ਼ ਨੂੰ ਉਸਦਾ ਪੁੱਤਰ ਬਲਵਿੰਦਰ ਸਿੰਘ ਲੈ ਕੇ ਆਇਆ ਹੈ। ਮਰੀਜ਼ ਗੁਰਮੀਤ ਕੌਰ ਹੈ, ਜੋ ਕਿ 73 ਸਾਲ ਦੀ ਮਹਿਲਾ ਹੈ ਅਤੇ ਰੂਪਨਗਰ ਜ਼ਿਲ੍ਹੇ ਦੇ ਪੇਂਡੂ ਪਿਛੋਕੜ ਤੋਂ ਹੈ। ਉਹ ਅਨਪੜ੍ਹ ਹੈ ਅਤੇ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਬੋਲਦੀ ਹੈ। ਉਹ 2 ਦਿਨਾਂ ਤੋਂ ਪੇਟ ਦੇ ਹੇਠਲੇ ਹਿੱਸੇ ਵਿੱਚ ਵਧਦੇ ਹੋਏ ਦਰਦ ਦੀ ਰਿਪੋਰਟ ਕਰਦੀ ਹੈ, ਜਿਸ ਦੇ ਨਾਲ ਜੀਅ ਕੱਚਾ ਹੋਣਾ ਅਤੇ ਦੋ ਵਾਰ ਬਿਨਾਂ ਪਿੱਤ ਵਾਲਾ ਉਲਟੀ ਆਉਣਾ ਸ਼ਾਮਲ ਹੈ। ਉਹ ਕਿਸੇ ਵੀ ਬੁਖਾਰ, ਕੰਬਣੀ, ਜਾਂ ਅੰਤੜੀਆਂ ਦੀਆਂ ਆਦਤਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਬਦਲਾਅ ਤੋਂ ਇਨਕਾਰ ਕਰਦੀ ਹੈ। ਉਸਨੇ ਦਰਦ ਲਈ ਜੜੀ-ਬੂਟੀਆਂ ਦੇ ਉਪਚਾਰ ਕੀਤੇ ਹਨ ਪਰ ਕੋਈ ਰਾਹਤ ਨਹੀਂ ਮਿਲੀ।

ਪਰੀਖਣ 'ਤੇ, ਮਰੀਜ਼ ਸੁਚੇਤ ਅਤੇ ਦਿਸ਼ਾ-ਨਿਰਦੇਸ਼ਿਤ ਹੈ। ਪੇਟ ਨਰਮ ਹੈ ਪਰ ਸੁਪਰਾਪਿਊਬਿਕ ਖੇਤਰ ਵਿੱਚ ਕੋਮਲ ਹੈ ਅਤੇ ਗਾਰਡਿੰਗ ਮੌਜੂਦ ਹੈ। ਅੰਤੜੀਆਂ ਦੀਆਂ ਆਵਾਜ਼ਾਂ ਹਾਈਪੋਐਕਟਿਵ ਹਨ। ਕੋਈ ਰੀਬਾਉਂਡ ਜਾਂ ਗਾਰਡਿੰਗ ਨਹੀਂ ਦੇਖੀ ਗਈ। ਕਾਰਡੀਓਵੈਸਕੁਲਰ ਪਰੀਖਣ ਤੋਂ ਪਤਾ ਲੱਗਦਾ ਹੈ ਕਿ ਟੈਕੀਕਾਰਡੀਆ ਹੈ। ਸਾਹ ਦੀ ਜਾਂਚ ਦੋਵਾਂ ਪਾਸਿਆਂ ਤੋਂ ਸਾਫ਼ ਹੈ। ਨਿਊਰੋਲੋਜੀਕਲ ਪਰੀਖਣ ਨਾਨ-ਫੋਕਲ ਹੈ।

Vitals ਹਰ 30 ਮਿੰਟਾਂ ਬਾਅਦ ਨਿਗਰਾਨੀ ਕੀਤੀ ਗਈ। BP 148/88 mmHg 'ਤੇ ਸਥਿਰ ਹੈ। HR 96 bpm. SpO2 97% ਰਹਿੰਦਾ ਹੈ। Temp 101.0°F. ਪਿਛਲੇ 2 ਘੰਟਿਆਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਪਿਸ਼ਾਬ ਦੀ ਮਾਤਰਾ 20 ml ਹੈ। ਮਰੀਜ਼ NPO ਹੈ। ਖੱਬੇ ਹੱਥ ਵਿੱਚ 18G ਕੈਨੁਲਾ ਨਾਲ ਇੱਕ IV ਲਾਈਨ ਸਥਾਪਤ ਕੀਤੀ ਗਈ ਹੈ। CBC, CMP, ਅਤੇ CRP ਲਈ ਖੂਨ ਦੇ ਨਮੂਨੇ ਭੇਜੇ ਗਏ ਹਨ। ਰੁਟੀਨ ਅਤੇ ਮਾਈਕ੍ਰੋਸਕੋਪੀ ਲਈ ਪਿਸ਼ਾਬ ਦਾ ਨਮੂਨਾ ਇਕੱਠਾ ਕੀਤਾ ਗਿਆ ਹੈ। ਮਰੀਜ਼ ਨੂੰ IV ਤਰਲ ਪਦਾਰਥ (ਨਾਰਮਲ ਸਲਾਈਨ) 100 ml/hour 'ਤੇ ਸ਼ੁਰੂ ਕੀਤਾ ਗਿਆ ਹੈ। ਦਰਦ ਪ੍ਰਬੰਧਨ ਲਈ paracetamol 500 mg IV ਦਿੱਤਾ ਗਿਆ ਹੈ। ਮਰੀਜ਼ ਅਗਲੇ ਮੁਲਾਂਕਣ ਅਤੇ ਪ੍ਰਬੰਧਨ ਦੀ ਉਡੀਕ ਕਰ ਰਿਹਾ ਹੈ।

Vitals / acuity: Patient is alert but appears fatigued. BP 150/90 mmHg, HR 98 bpm, RR 20/min, SpO2 96% on room air, Temp 101.2°F. Pain score 6/10 in lower abdomen. Patient reports feeling dizzy and weak.

Nursing Notes:
Patient brought in by son Balwinder Singh. Patient is Gurmeet Kaur, a 73-year-old female from a rural background in Rupnagar district. She is illiterate and speaks Punjabi. She reports a 2-day history of progressive lower abdominal pain, associated with nausea and two episodes of non-bilious vomiting. She denies any fever, chills, or changes in bowel habits. She has been taking herbal remedies for the pain without relief.

On examination, patient is conscious and oriented. Abdomen is soft but tender in the suprapubic region with guarding. Bowel sounds are hypoactive. No rebound or guarding noted. Cardiovascular examination reveals tachycardia. Respiratory examination is clear bilaterally. Neurological examination is non-focal.

Vitals monitored every 30 minutes. BP stable at 148/88 mmHg. HR 96 bpm. SpO2 remains 97%. Temp 101.0°F. Urine output is 20 ml over the last 2 hours. Patient is NPO. An IV line has been established with a 18G cannula in the left forearm. Blood samples have been sent for CBC, CMP, and CRP. A urine sample has been collected for routine and microscopy. Patient has been started on IV fluids (Normal Saline) at 100 ml/hour. Pain management with paracetamol 500 mg IV has been administered. The patient is awaiting further evaluation and management.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sanskrit (code=sa, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Kanpur Nagar
- first_language: Sanskrit
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh, with a matriculation education, currently not employed. He resides in an urban setting and speaks Sanskrit as his first language, with functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes occasional anxiety‑driven yoga, a preference for street food, and a cultural background that blends Islamic traditions with an appreciation for classical Indian arts.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
ASHA टिप्पणी — ग्राम [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]], जनपद [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
लाभार्थी [[PATIENT_NAME|Abdul Rahim]], [[AGE|19]] / [[GENDER|Male]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]] | दूरभाष [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
भ्रमण निष्कर्ष: रोगी त्रिसप्ताहं यावत् निरन्तरं कासं तथा अल्पज्वरं सूचयति। सः श्रान्तः अनुभवति तथा च अद्यतनकाले प्रायः द्विकिलोपरिमाणं भारं नष्टवान्। तस्य पिता [[RELATIVE_NAME|Mohammed Yusuf]], चिन्तितः अस्ति तथा च भ्रमणार्थं सह आगच्छति। रोगी [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]] युक्तः BPL कार्डधारकः अस्ति। सः [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]] मध्ये पञ्जीकृतः अस्ति तथा च [[RELIGION|Muslim]] इति परिचयं ददाति। रोगी अविवाहितः अस्ति तथा च वर्तमानकाले बेरोजगारः अस्ति। सः कानपुर-नगरे नगरीयक्षेत्रे वसति तथा च गृहे संस्कृतं भाषते। वयं TB परीक्षणार्थं जनपद-चिकित्सालये कफ-नमूना-सङ्ग्रहस्य व्यवस्थां कृतवन्तः। रोगी एकसप्ताहं यावत् अनुवर्तनं करिष्यते येन परीक्षणपरिणामानां परीक्षणं भवेत् तथा च यदि निदानं भवति तर्हि उपचारः आरब्धः इति सुनिश्चितं भवेत्।

Visit findings: Patient reports persistent cough for three weeks and low-grade evening fevers. He feels fatigued and has lost about two kilograms recently. His father, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Mohammed Yusuf]], is concerned and accompanies him for the visit. The patient is a BPL cardholder with [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]. He is registered with [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]] and identifies as [[RELIGION|Muslim]]. The patient is unmarried and currently not employed. He lives in an urban area of Kanpur Nagar and speaks Sanskrit at home. We have arranged for a sputum sample collection at the District Hospital for TB screening. The patient will be followed up in one week to check on the test results and ensure he has started his treatment if diagnosed.

रोगी के पास नाभि के चारों ओर दर्द का 2 दिन का इतिहास है जो दाहिने निचले चतुर्थांश में स्थानांतरित हो गया है, साथ ही मतली और हल्का बुखार है।

पेट नरम है जिसमें दाहिने इलियक फोसा में कोमलता और गार्डिंग है। रिबाउंड कोमलता मौजूद है। आंतों की ध्वनियाँ कम हो गई हैं। कोई अंगवृद्धि नहीं।

WBC गणना 14,500 कोशिकाएं/mm3 है। पेट का अल्ट्रासाउंड एक गैर-संपीड्य अपेंडिक्स को पेरी-अपेंडिसील द्रव के साथ दिखाता है।

सर्जिकल मूल्यांकन के लिए भर्ती किया गया। रोगी की आपातकालीन अपेंडेक्टोमी निर्धारित है। सहमति प्राप्त की गई। रोगी को NPO स्थिति की सलाह दी गई। प्री-ऑपरेटिव एंटीबायोटिक्स दिए गए।

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

arts_persona: Meena Devi's artistic flair shines through vibrant Jhumar dance performances, intricately crafted bamboo‑cane ornaments, colorful rangoli designs during festivals, and a love for Bollywood classics starring Shah Rukh Khan and music by Arijit Singh.

persona: Meena Devi’s inquisitive charm, disciplined work ethic, and love for folk rhythm make them a reliable telemarketing leader who hums Jhumar tunes while juggling call schedules and craft aspirations.
cultural_background: Meena belongs to the Santhal tribal community of Dumka, Jharkhand. Though she identifies as Hindu, her cultural life is steeped in Santhal traditions – folk songs, dances, and festivals like Sohrai and Baha, blended with mainstream Hindu rituals observed in the villages. Rural life, agrarian cycles, and close‑knit village networks shape her worldview.
linguistic_background: Meena’s mother tongue is Santali, which she speaks fluently and uses in daily conversation with her family and community. She is also conversationally comfortable in Hindi, enough to understand customers and follow telemarketing scripts. Due to her illiterate background, she cannot read or write in either Santali or Hindi, relying on oral instruction and visual cues for work.
skills_and_expertise: She excels at voice‑based customer interaction, persuasive sales communication, handling objections, maintaining telemarketing records, and using basic phone and computer interfaces. Her organized nature helps her keep track of call logs and follow‑up schedules. She also has a keen eye for folk art and crafts, which she occasionally leverages for community events.

hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Santhali Jhumar dance", "Bamboo and cane crafts", "Rangoli painting", "Gardening", "Traditional Jharkhand cooking", "Bollywood movies", "Community art workshops"]

She wishes to improve her Hindi literacy to read scripts independently, and eventually transition into a training role where she can mentor new recruits. Long‑term, she hopes to secure a stable income that enables her to support her extended family and invest in a small home‑based craft business.

Her lifestyle includes a disciplined work ethic and a love for folk rhythms, which she hums while managing call schedules.

ER triage — District Hospital Dumka Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-004]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]] [[AGE|32]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|07]]
Arrived by ambulance vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|JH07C-1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431023456]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sunita Murmu]]
Vitals / acuity: GCS 15, BP 140/90, HR 110, RR 22, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 37.8°C. Patient is alert, oriented, but appears anxious and is moaning intermittently. Reports severe lower abdominal pain with uterine contractions every 2-3 minutes, lasting 45-60 seconds. Bleeding noted on perineal pad, estimated 200ml. Fetal movement is reduced.

Nursing Notes:
Patient Meena Devi, a 32-year-old female, presents via ambulance with chief complaint of severe abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding. She is G1P0, currently married, and works as a telemarketing sales person. Her vitals are stable but tachycardic. On abdominal examination, the uterus is firm, tender, and contracting frequently. A sterile perineal pad shows moderate amount of bright red blood. Fetal heart rate is 145 bpm with occasional decelerations noted on the monitor. The patient is anxious and requests pain relief. She is illiterate and speaks Santali, so communication is being facilitated through a family member who is conversant in Hindi. An IV line of 18G has been established in the left forearm with normal saline running at 100ml/hour. A Foley catheter has been inserted, and initial urine output is 30ml. The patient has been instructed to breathe through contractions. Dr. Sunita Murmu has been notified and is en route to the bedside. A STAT complete blood count, blood group and crossmatch, and coagulation profile have been sent to the lab. The patient is being prepared for possible emergency cesarean section.

professional_persona: Jashodaben Makvana, a retired homemaker turned community resource coordinator, applies her methodical budgeting, extensive knowledge of regional cuisine, and organized event‑planning skills to manage family finances and volunteer for temple festivals, while they confidently lead workshops for fellow homemakers.
linguistic_persona: Jashodaben Makvana speaks Sindhi at home, is fluent in Gujarati for everyday interactions, converses comfortably in Hindi, and can read simple texts in both Sindhi and Gujarati scripts, though they have only basic literacy in Hindi.
sports_persona: Jashodaben Makvana stays active by joining the neighbourhood Garba troupe during Navratri, walking briskly in the morning park, avidly following the Gujarat Titans in the IPL and supporting the local cricket team, while they prefer low‑impact exercise and avoid high‑intensity gym routines.
arts_persona: Jashodaben Makvana immerses herself in the arts by listening to Anup Jalota’s bhajans, regional folk tunes and community devotional songs, regularly dancing Garba to the rhythm of local bands, and occasionally attending theatre productions starring Pravin Pandya, while they cherish these cultural expressions as a source of joy and community bonding.
travel_persona: Jashodaben Makvana prefers well‑planned, modest trips, often visiting the Rann of Kutch for the full‑moon festival, taking weekend pilgrimages to Somnath and Dwarka, enjoying annual shopping excursions to Delhi during Diwali, and dreaming of a future family vacation to Dubai’s souks, while they balance travel costs with household budgeting.
culinary_persona: Jashodaben Makvana is a seasoned home cook who expertly prepares regional kadhi, bhindi sabzi, dhokla, khandvi and handvo, delights in making tangy mango and lemon pickles using mustard oil, fenugreek seeds and raw mangoes, and enjoys sharing these dishes at family gatherings, while they occasionally experiment with fusion twists but stay rooted in traditional flavors.
persona: Jashodaben Makvana blends meticulous budgeting, a passion for regional cooking, and a love for lively community festivals into a disciplined yet sociable lifestyle, though they sometimes forget to take their medication on busy days.
cultural_background: Jashodaben comes from a Sindhi Hindu family that settled in Jamnagar, Gujarat after the Partition of India; growing up in the city’s vibrant urban setting, she blends Sindhi traditions such as Cheti Chand celebrations with Gujarati customs like Garba during Navratri, and participates actively in family rituals and community gatherings at the local mandir.
linguistic_background: Jashodaben's mother tongue is Sindhi, which she uses at home and in community events; she is equally fluent in Gujarati for everyday interactions, market visits, and handling local paperwork; with education only below primary, her reading and writing skills are basic, allowing her to read simple texts in both Sindhi and Gujarati scripts and converse easily in Hindi, though she is not formally literate in Hindi.
skills_and_expertise: Jashodaben excels in household management, meticulously planning meals, finances and chores with a methodical approach; she is proficient in cooking a wide array of Sindhi and Gujarati dishes, balancing traditional flavors with the practical needs of her family; her organizational abilities extend to community event coordination, where she volunteers for festivals, Garba gatherings and temple functions; she maintains an accurate family budget despite limited formal education and is adept at basic first‑aid and health care for her household; additionally, her interpersonal skills enable her to mediate minor family disputes and foster cooperation among neighbors.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Sindhi & Gujarati cooking", "Community event coordination", "Family budgeting", "Basic first aid", "Mediation and interpersonal communication"]
hobbies_and_interests: Jashodaben enjoys cooking and sharing traditional Sindhi and Gujarati meals, which lets her combine culinary skill with social interaction. She actively participates in Garba and Dandiya during Navratri, finding joy in dance and community spirit. She tends a small courtyard garden, growing herbs and flowers used in cooking. She loves listening to bhajans and folk songs on the radio and attending temple discourses to deepen her religious knowledge. Additionally, she knits scarves and stitches simple garments for her grandchildren and sometimes joins local women’s devotional book clubs.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Cooking traditional Sindhi and Gujarati meals", "Participating in Garba and Dandiya", "Gardening and herb cultivation", "Listening to bhajans and folk songs", "Attending temple discourses", "Knitting scarves and stitching simple garments", "Joining women’s devotional book clubs"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Although Jashodaben has retired from formal employment, she wishes to deepen her expertise in traditional Sindhi‑Gujarati cooking, possibly by compiling a small family recipe booklet. She aims to take a more active role in the local women's forum, organising workshops on budgeting and basic health practices for other homemakers. Over the next few years she plans to launch a modest home‑based venture, such as preparing and selling pickles and snacks during local festivals, thereby supplementing the family income while preserving culinary heritage.

Her first language is Sindhi, with fluency in Gujarati for daily interactions. She has below primary education and manages household finances while engaging in community festival activities. Clinically, she demonstrates disciplined budgeting but may occasionally forget medication during busy periods.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

- sex / gender: Female
- age: 46
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Gujarat
- district: Jamnagar
- first_language: Sindhi

Clinically, she demonstrates disciplined budgeting but may occasionally forget medication during busy periods.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:

[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]

Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
